title: post_shift_overrun_penalty applied to final_score but missing from MIN_PROPOSE gate's ranking-delta exclusion tuple (recreates the INV-GATE-SCORE-DELTA 'była luka')

The KOORD silence gate uses `_best_score_gate = _gate_score_excluding_ranking_deltas(top[0])` (6326), which subtracts back ONLY the 7 entries in `_GATE_RANKING_DELTA_EXCLUSIONS` (2307-2315): sync_spread, loadgov, r1_progressive, v319h_guard, repo_cost, bundle_fit, fix_c. post_shift is NOT among them. NIGDY nie wpycha decyzji w ciszę' and explicitly calls r1_progressive/v319h 'były LUKĄ' — the same defect for post_shift. Both post_shift (common.py:136-140 registry note 'Adrian 2026-06-24') and the exclusion tuple are dated the same 2026-06-24 sprint, and the tuple even lists OFF flags 'preemptive' — so post_shift is the forgotten preemptive entry.

protocol_note: When adding ANY decision_flag-gated penalty that is subtracted from final_score (the 5073-5093 block + 5057), it MUST also be added to _GATE_RANKING_DELTA_EXCLUSIONS (2307) per invariant INV-GATE-SCORE-DELTA, else a future flip re-introduces the SOFT-into-silence regression. verification.reasoning: Structural facts all verified true: post_shift_overrun_penalty is subtracted from final_score under ENABLE_POST_SHIFT_OVERRUN_PENALTY (dispatch_pipeline.py:5057-5058), is serialized in metrics (5431, key post_shift_overrun_penalty), and is absent from _GATE_RANKING_DELTA_EXCLUSIONS (2307-2315). The guard test_inv_gate_score_delta does NOT cover it: I ran its own regex (matches only 'final_score = final_score + var') over live source via the dispatch venv; it caught the 7 additive deltas, missed post_shift (subtractive, with extra 'and' clause). So the INV-GATE-SCORE-DELTA coverage gap is real. BUT impact is doubly dormant: (1) ENABLE_POST_SHIFT_OVERRUN_PENALTY is env-default '0' and absent from flags.json (common.py:1800-1801), so it never touches final_score today; (2) the KOORD silence gate it would feed is bypassed by 'and not _always_propose_on()' (6330) because ENABLE_ALWAYS_PROPOSE_ON_SATURATION=true in flags.json (line 184) and the all_candidates_low_score branch is currently dead. title: O2 overage/cap-Z fixed at 35, not escalation-aware (T3=40), and split across two constants
evidence: O2_OVERAGE_CAP_MIN = float(env 'O2_OVERAGE_CAP_MIN','35') (common.py:2562). route_simulator:776 passes this flat 35 to _compute_o2_metrics overage. plan_recheck:681 reads O2_CAP_Z_MIN (default 35) separately for over_z. Meanwhile the live hard escalation (feas_carry_readmit / best_effort, BEST_EFFORT_OBJM_NEW_ORDER_CAP_MIN=40 in flags.json) allows a Tier-3 stretch to 40min.
impact: OFF today (sweep flag off) so no live effect. On the 02.07 flip the overage objective will penalize the 35–40 band that the hard escalation path legitimately permits for T3, biasing selection against the very stretch the engine is allowed to make. Two independent cap constants (O2_OVERAGE_CAP_MIN in route_simulator vs O2_CAP_Z_MIN in plan_recheck) can drift if only one is tuned at flip time — config-twin hazard.
protocol_note: At O2 flip (02.07) decide whether overage cap should be escalation/tier-aware (35 normal, 40 T3 stretch) to stay consistent with BEST_EFFORT_OBJM_NEW_ORDER_CAP_MIN=40; and tune O2_OVERAGE_CAP_MIN and O2_CAP_Z_MIN together (twin) or unify them. MEMORY note already says flip = 'stroj cap-Z z under_z' — fold the 35-vs-40 escalation question into that tuning.
recommendation: During the calibrated flip, evaluate under_z collector data to set cap-Z; explicitly decide flat-35 vs tier-aware-40 for overage and keep the two constants in lockstep.

verification.reasoning: Factual observations are accurate (common.py:2562-2563 both default 35; route_simulator_v2.py:776 passes flat O2_OVERAGE_CAP_MIN; plan_recheck.py:681/687 reads O2_CAP_Z_MIN separately; ENABLE_O2_READY_ANCHOR_SWEEP OFF, absent from flags.json), but the load-bearing thesis is wrong. (1) o2_score is a CONTINUOUS soft staleness-minimization objective (_compute_o2_metrics route_simulator_v2.py:731-757: overage = sum of max(0, age-cap)) that only orders route SEQUENCES within one courier's bag (_plan_from_sequence / _pick_best l.139-160). It never selects WHICH courier nor gates feasibility, so it cannot bias selection against a T3 stretch -- the hard escalation (R6 35/40, best_effort, feas_carry_readmit) lives in a separate feasibility layer O2 does not override. Penalizing the 35-40 band uniformly is exactly correct for a minimize-overage objective and orthogonal to a hard allow-up-to-40 gate; expecting the soft objective to be tier-aware like R6 conflates the two layers (the inverse of tier-aware-R6-mistaken-for-a-bug). (2) cap-Z never blocks a necessary carry: l.148-152 _under_z falls back to full pool when every plan exceeds Z (least-bad), only preferring fresher sequences when one exists. (3) The config-twin hazard is by-design independence: common.py:2557-2563 documents O2_OVERAGE_CAP_MIN and O2_CAP_Z_MIN as semantically distinct knobs with different data sources and different candidate values (overage 35/40, cap-Z candidates 20/32/35), explicitly slated for separate 02.07 data tuning -- not an accidental split. (4) The scalar-35 is a documented pre-flip placeholder, with the comment itself naming the T3=40 rare-day case; this is the in-progress O2 sprint behind an OFF flag, not standalone tech-debt. No genuine contradiction or live risk.

title: same-restaurant grouping double-inserts the super-pickup node in greedy/bruteforce paths (masked by OR-Tools ON; un-masked by documented 1-flag rollback)
evidence: In simulate_bag_route_v2 grouping branch, all grouped oids map to ONE super-pickup index: `for grp_oid in grp_key: bag_pickup_idxs_by_oid[grp_oid] = super_pickup_idx` (l.352-353). _bruteforce_plan then does `to_place.extend(bag_pickup_idxs_by_oid.values())` (l.803) → for a 2-order group this appends the SAME index twice → permutations visit that node twice (double dwell + extra leg, pickup_at overwritten by 2nd visit). _greedy_plan builds `pending_pairs = [(bag_pickup_idxs_by_oid[oid], d_idx) ...]` (l.855-859) → [(3,dA),(3,dB)]; Step 1.5 inserts pickup idx 3 once per pair (l.880-902) → node 3 inserted twice. _ortools_plan is correct (it adds two AddPickupAndDelivery pairs sharing one pickup node, visited once). Grouping requires ≥2 pending bag items → bag_after_add≥3 → with ENABLE_V326_OR_TOOLS_TSP ON and V327_MIN_OR_TOOLS_BAG_AFTER=2 it ALWAYS routes to _ortools_plan, so the greedy/bruteforce dup is currently unreachable.
impact: Latent correctness bug: a grouped same-restaurant bag routed through greedy/bruteforce gets an inflated total_duration (extra pickup dwell + leg) and pickup_at stamped at the later visit → later predicted deliveries → wrong sequence selection / R6/SLA mis-count. CLAUDE.md documents the emergency rollback as `ENABLE_V326_OR_TOOLS_TSP=False (1 flaga = full rollback do legacy)` while leaving ENABLE_V326_SAME_RESTAURANT_GROUPING=True → that exact rollback activates this bug during an incident, the worst time.
protocol_note: Future session: the OR-Tools-OFF rollback path is NOT behaviorally equivalent to legacy when grouping is ON — greedy/bruteforce double-visit grouped super-pickups. If you ever flip ENABLE_V326_OR_TOOLS_TSP off, ALSO flip ENABLE_V326_SAME_RESTAURANT_GROUPING off, or dedup bag_pickup_idxs_by_oid.values() in _bruteforce_plan.to_place and skip already-inserted super-pickup in _greedy_plan Step 1.5.
recommendation: Read-only verify: construct a 2-order same-restaurant pending bag, set ENABLE_V326_OR_TOOLS_TSP=0 in a scratch repro (NOT prod), call simulate_bag_route_v2, assert the produced sequence node-index list contains the super-pickup index exactly once. Confirm fix needs dedup in both twin paths.

verification.reasoning:
The double-insert of the super-pickup node in both legacy planners is real and verified. Grouping pre-pass maps every grouped oid to ONE super_pickup_idx (route_simulator_v2.py:352-353), so bag_pickup_idxs_by_oid={oidA:3,oidB:3}. _bruteforce_plan does to_place.extend(bag_pickup_idxs_by_oid.values()) (l.803) yielding [3,3] in permutations; the precedence guard at l.838 uses perm.index(p_idx) which only sees the FIRST 3, so the second visit is unconstrained. _greedy_plan builds pending_pairs=[(3,dA),(3,dB)] (l.855-859) and Step 1.5 (l.876-905) inserts pickup idx 3 once per pair so 3 appears twice in seq_base. _simulate_sequence (l.557-583) walks seq with NO dedup: a repeated pickup node incurs a second drive leg plus a second dwell_pickup and re-stamps pickup_at[group_oids] at the later visit, giving inflated total_duration and a later pickup_at that propagates to later deliveries and miscounts R6/SLA. Dup and harm both confirmed.

On reachability the finding is slightly UNDERSTATED, not overstated. Grouping needs >=2 pending grouped orders (l.311,318) so bag_after_add>=3; with ENABLE_V326_OR_TOOLS_TSP default-ON (common.py:2257-2258, absent from flags.json) and V327_MIN_OR_TOOLS_BAG_AFTER=2 (l.2270), grouped bags normally route to _ortools_plan (l.460), masking the bug. The finding got that right. BUT _ortools_plan returns None on INFEASIBLE/no-sequence/timeout (l.969,1379,1388) and the caller then falls back to _greedy_plan (l.473) passing the SAME grouped dict, so the greedy dup fires on the live fleet today (rarely), i.e. it is NOT strictly unreachable as claimed. The documented 1-flag rollback ENABLE_V326_OR_TOOLS_TSP=False (grouping stays default-ON, common.py:3060-3061) un-masks it broadly: bag_after_add=3 routes to _bruteforce_plan (BRUTEFORCE_MAX_BAG_AFTER=3, l.36) and >3 to _greedy_plan. This is a genuine twin-path partial fix: only _ortools_plan handles the shared super-pickup correctly (one AddPickupAndDelivery pair per oid sharing one pickup node). title: OSRM fallback path double-counts traffic: traffic-aware bucket speed AND get_traffic_multiplier both applied
evidence: _haversine_fallback (osrm_client.py:376) computes duration_s = road_km / get_fallback_speed_kmh(now) * 3600, and FALLBACK_BASE_SPEEDS_KMH is explicitly traffic-jam-based (common.py:455 'Buckety prędkości oparte na KORKACH' weekday_rush=20 vs off_peak=32 = ~1.6x traffic spread). route()/table() then return _apply_traffic_multiplier(_haversine_fallback(...)) (osrm_client.py:581,593,626 and _table_fallback:789), which in LIVE mode multiplies duration_s by get_traffic_multiplier(now) (e.g. 1.55 at 15-17). _apply_traffic_multiplier has NO osrm_fallback guard (grep: osrm_fallback only at 382/539/615/768, never checked in the multiplier). So fallback gets traffic applied twice (once via slow bucket speed already encoding rush, once via the multiplier). The code comment at osrm_client.py:782 ('fallback path is consistent with main OSRM path') is the defect — the OSRM base is FREE-FLOW (common.py:496-500) but the fallback base is already traffic-adjusted, so the same multiplier is not consistent for fallback. Twin: dispatch_pipeline.py:3905-3907 drive_min exception fallback does the same (drive_min = dist/fleet_speed_kmh*60 where fleet_speed_kmh=get_fallback_speed_kmh, then *= C.get_traffic_multiplier(now)).
impact: During an OSRM outage at peak (circuit open or HTTP fail), every route/table leg over-estimates drive time by ~1.5-1.6x. Inflated drive_min feeds R6 (ready-anchored ≤35 thermal) and committed/ETA → spurious R6 breaches, more KOORD/best-effort-ALERT and demoted candidates exactly when the system is already degraded. Production fallback=0 in recent hours (OSRM healthy, circuit breaker + 60-min cache), so this is LATENT — but it amplifies harm at the worst moment. Not previously documented (TECH_DEBT/KB double-count refs are the unrelated tier-speed+DWELL one, OBALONY).
protocol_note: Any change to traffic multiplier or fallback speeds must treat the fallback base as ALREADY traffic-adjusted: either apply get_traffic_multiplier only to FREE-FLOW OSRM durations (skip when osrm_fallback=True / coord_invalid=True) OR rebase FALLBACK_BASE_SPEEDS to free-flow and let the multiplier do traffic. Fix must touch both twins (osrm_client fallback + dispatch_pipeline:3905-3907) and re-run a degraded-mode replay (force circuit open) to prove ON≠OFF.
recommendation: Next session: in shadow only, force circuit-open (or call _haversine_fallback directly) at a peak timestamp and compare fallback duration_min vs a known OSRM*multiplier baseline to quantify the over-estimate; decide between guarding _apply_traffic_multiplier on osrm_fallback/coord_invalid (cleanest) vs rebasing fallback speeds. Do NOT flip anything live — fallback is rarely exercised, so verify via unit/replay, not production.

verification.reasoning:
Re-read every cited site; the mechanism is real and unguarded.

1) Fallback base IS already congestion-adjusted. common.py:454-461: FALLBACK_BASE_SPEEDS_KMH carries the explicit comment "Buckety predkosci oparte na KORKACH (nie na popycie)" with weekday_rush=20 vs off_peak=32 (~1.6x). The speed variation is attributed to traffic jams, so weekday_rush=20 km/h is an effective CONGESTED speed, not free-flow. get_fallback_speed_kmh (common.py:516) returns exactly that bucket value.

2) _haversine_fallback (osrm_client.py:370-388) computes duration_s = road_km / get_fallback_speed_kmh(now) * 3600 and tags osrm_fallback=True, but does NOT set osrm_raw_duration_s.

3) route()/table() wrap every fallback in _apply_traffic_multiplier: lines 581, 593, 626 and _table_fallback:789. I read _apply_traffic_multiplier in full (293-368): raw_s = result.get('osrm_raw_duration_s', result.get('duration_s')) so for a fallback dict that is the congested duration. There is NO osrm_fallback guard in the function; in LIVE mode (355-366) duration_s = raw_s * get_traffic_multiplier(now), e.g. x1.55 weekday 15-17. So the congestion encoded in the 20 km/h bucket is applied a second time.

Magnitude check confirms double-count: OSRM free-flow (~32 km/h) x1.55 ~= 20.6 km/h effective at rush, which already matches the fallback bucket (20 km/h); multiplying the fallback again yields ~13 km/h, i.e. ~1.55x over-estimate. Off-peak is self-consistent only because both bucket (32) and multiplier (1.0) sit at neutral; the divergence appears exactly at peak. The 'fallback path consistent with main OSRM path' comment (osrm_client.py:782) is the defect: OSRM base is free-flow, fallback base is congested, so the same multiplier is NOT consistent.

Live-state check: ENABLE_V326_OSRM_TRAFFIC_MULTIPLIER defaults to '1' (common.py:3229-3230); no env override or flags.json entry in dispatch-shadow.service or drop-ins (grep of /etc/systemd/system and flags.json both empty), and MEMORY confirms V326 multiplier recalibrated/live 05-06. So the multiplier IS applied live; the double-count is real whenever fallback fires.

Twin confirmed: dispatch_pipeline.py:3427 fleet_speed_kmh = get_fallback_speed_kmh(now); 3905-3909 drive_min = road_km/fleet_speed_kmh*60 then *= C.get_traffic_multiplier(now) -- same structure. But this twin only runs on a hard exception from osrm_client.route() (3894-3902), and route() itself already handles circuit-open via haversine fallback, so the twin is even rarer.

Severity: confirmed bug but LATENT -- fallback ~0 in healthy operation (OSRM up, circuit breaker + cache). Harm is conditional on an OSRM outage, and is then largest exactly at peak (~1.5x inflated drive_min feeding R6 ready-anchored cap, committed/ETA -> spurious breaches, more KOORD/best-effort-ALERT). P2 is defensible because of the amplify-at-worst-moment property; P3 is arguable purely on rarity. I keep P2. Not a tier-aware-R6 false alarm and not an always-propose sentinel; it is a genuine free-flow-vs-congested base-speed inconsistency (partial-change anti-pattern: multiplier bolted onto a fallback whose base was never re-baselined to free-flow).
evidence_checked: osrm_client.py:293-368 (_apply_traffic_multiplier, no osrm_fallback guard, raw_s from duration_s)

title: App renders route_podjazdy (force carried-first) while panel renders relaxed canon — courier vs coordinator see different stop order
evidence: plan_recheck.py:1497 applies CARRIED_FIRST_RELAX in _apply_canon_order_invariants — the engine can intentionally place a carried (picked_up) dropoff AFTER a colocated pickup ("odbierz po drodze zanim dowieziesz niesione"). fleet_state.py:320-323 _build_canon_route renders canon order verbatim including "carried-first relax silnika" when `flag("TRUST_CANON_ORDER")` (PANEL_FLAG_TRUST_CANON_ORDER=1 LIVE confirmed via systemctl show nadajesz-panel.service). BUT the app: courier_orders.py:1105 `if config.APP_ROUTE_FROM_CONSOLE and _route_podjazdy is not None and mine:` (ENABLE_APP_ROUTE_FROM_CONSOLE=1 LIVE via systemctl show courier-api.service) calls route_podjazdy.order_podjazdy. route_podjazdy.py:153-156 unconditionally pulls ALL carried dropoffs to the absolute front (`carried = sorted(...); order = [("dropoff",[oid]) for o in carried]`) with no relax — structurally cannot place a carried dropoff after a pickup. So in any relax case the app shows [deliver carried A, pickup B, deliver B] while the panel shows [pickup B, deliver A, deliver B].
impact: Violates route_podjazdy's own stated goal ("koordynator i kurier widzą DOKŁADNIE to samo"). The courier app silently reverts the engine's carried-first-relax optimization (the very inefficiency the relax was built to remove), and disagrees with the coordinator console, in the relax scenario.
protocol_note: route_podjazdy.order_podjazdy is NOT canon-relax-aware; it is a parallel re-derivation, not a verbatim canon render. When changing carried-first/relax in the engine, the app surface (courier_orders via route_podjazdy) and panel surface (fleet_state._build_canon_route) are TWIN paths that must be updated together — they currently disagree.
recommendation: Verify frequency of CARRIED_FIRST_RELAX applied (grep CARRIED_FIRST_RELAX in logs/plan_recheck.log) to scope materiality, then either make route_podjazdy honor the relaxed plan stop order (render canon verbatim when plan covers bag, mirroring fleet_state TRUST_CANON) or document the app as intentionally non-relaxed.

title: Latent dual-writer clobber on pending_proposals.json — mutual exclusion enforced only by telegram being disabled, no code interlock
evidence: telegram_approver.save_pending (1759-1766) writes the WHOLE in-memory dict atomically with no fcntl/cross-process lock: `os.replace(tmp, path)`. state['pending'] is seeded once at startup (4264 `"pending": load_pending(PENDING_PATH)`) then full-overwritten on every send/pop (2088, 4017). Meanwhile shadow_dispatcher writes the SAME file via pending_proposals_store.upsert_proposals which does load→sweep→merge→save (pending_proposals_store.py:100-105), gated by `_pp_write = bool(C.flag('ENABLE_PENDING_PROPOSALS_WRITE', False))` (shadow:1105) — flags.json shows it true. pending_proposals_store.py docstring (lines 11-14) states the no-race invariant explicitly: 'Pisarz: shadow_dispatcher (jedyny po wyłączeniu Telegrama/postpone → brak wyścigu pisarzy)'. dispatch-telegram is currently `inactive`+`disabled` (verified via systemctl), so only shadow writes — safe NOW. But there is NO code that disables ENABLE_PENDING_PROPOSALS_WRITE when telegram comes up; the two writers are independent flags.
impact: If a future session re-enables dispatch-telegram while ENABLE_PENDING_PROPOSALS_WRITE stays true, both processes write pending_proposals.json with no shared lock. telegram's save_pending full-overwrites with its stale startup snapshot, dropping every shadow-written entry added after telegram startup (and shadow's next merge re-adds, telegram clobbers again → thrash + lost updates). Consumers of the shadow-written file (panel_watcher PANEL_OVERRIDE detection, _save_plan_from_pending, tools/pending_global_resweep) lose entries. Additionally telegram would seed shadow entries that carry message_id=None (pending_proposals_store.build_entry:80) into state['pending']; the postpone handler (3918 entry['message_id']) and editMessageReplyMarkup (3971) would then push message_id=None to the Telegram API.
protocol_note: Add to Przykazanie #0 / change-protocol: re-enabling dispatch-telegram is NOT flag-neutral — it must be paired with flipping ENABLE_PENDING_PROPOSALS_WRITE=false (or adding a real cross-process lock to both writers), otherwise pending_proposals.json gets clobbered. The 'no race' invariant is operational convention, not code-enforced.
recommendation: Next session: before any telegram re-enable, verify ENABLE_PENDING_PROPOSALS_WRITE state and treat telegram-on + write-flag-on as mutually exclusive. Longer-term fix (via protocol): give both writers a shared fcntl.LOCK_EX on a lock file and make telegram's save_pending do load→merge→save instead of full-overwrite, OR have shadow stop writing the moment telegram is live. Verify panel_watcher consumers tolerate message_id=None entries.

title: watchdog expired-loop uses direct index state['pending'][oid] (no guard) where its startup-scan twin uses .get()+continue — KeyError can kill the watchdog coroutine
evidence: watchdog() builds `expired` (4203-4210), then `await state_machine.get_all()` (4214-4216, yields the event loop), then loops `for oid in expired: entry = state['pending'][oid]` (4219-4221) — direct index, NO per-iteration try/except. The structurally identical startup-scan twin _startup_scan_pending_expired loops `entry = state['pending'].get(oid); if not entry: continue` (4144-4146) — defensive. If handle_callback (which pops at 4016) or a prior _process_expired_pending iteration (pops at 4082/4101) removes pending[oid] during the get_all() await, the watchdog's `state['pending'][oid]` raises KeyError. That KeyError is unhandled inside watchdog's loop body; watchdog is launched via asyncio.gather (4283), so an unhandled exception there propagates.
impact: Rare interleave (an entry must be expired exactly when a concurrent callback/sweep pops it during the get_all() yield) crashes the watchdog coroutine → 5-min auto-KOORD timeout safety net stops firing until service restart. Currently dormant (dispatch-telegram off). Note: the worried-about double gastro_assign does NOT occur because the watchdog TIMEOUT path (4085+) does not call run_gastro_assign — it only sends a message and pops; so the concrete fallout is the KeyError crash, not a double-assign.
recommendation: Next session (via protocol, telegram-touch = ACK gate): change watchdog 4220 to `entry = state['pending'].get(oid); if not entry: continue` to match the startup-scan twin, eliminating the KeyError window. Pure robustness, no behavior change on the happy path.

verification.reasoning: Twin asymmetry is real: watchdog line 4220 indexes state['pending'][oid] with no guard, while the startup-scan twin at 4144-4146 uses .get(oid) then 'if not entry: continue'. A KeyError could in principle propagate (no try/except around 4219-4221; watchdog has no outer guard; gather at 4283-4289 has no return_exceptions=True). BUT the finding's PRIMARY cited race window is factually wrong: it claims 'await state_machine.get_all() (4214-4216) yields the event loop'. The actual code at line 4216 is 'state_all = state_machine.get_all()' — a SYNCHRONOUS call, NOT awaited (identical to startup-scan line 4140). No yield occurs there, so the first loop iteration is always safe and a concurrent pop CANNOT occur 'during the get_all() await' as described. The only real yield is 'await _process_expired_pending' (4221), and within it 'await asyncio.to_thread(tg_request,...)' at 4086 — and ONLY on the TIMEOUT branch (the SUPERSEDED branch 4067-4084 returns with no await). So KeyError is reachable only on iteration N>=2, requiring 2+ entries expiring in the same 10s tick, the first taking the TIMEOUT path, and a concurrent handle_callback (pop at 4016) firing during that send — a far narrower/rarer window than stated. The bug thesis (unguarded index vs guarded twin, unhandled KeyError) holds; the cited mechanism does not. Impact correctly de-scoped (no double-assign; all pops are .pop(oid,None) defensive) and dormant (dispatch-telegram intentionally muted). P3 is fair: rare interleave, dormant, watchdog is itself a backstop safety net. Verdict partial because the finding's evidence misidentifies the yield point.

title: last-known-pos TTL measured from tick save-time, not observation-time → rescued position staleness understated (can exceed ~50min while age field shows <=25min)
evidence: Save block writes `_updates[kid] = {"lat":..,"lon":..,"ts": _now_iso, "source": cs.pos_source}` (line 1223-1226) using the CURRENT tick time, regardless of how old the underlying observation was. A step-3 recent-activity source (last_delivered/last_picked_up_recent) is accepted with real `best_age` up to RECENT_MAX_MIN=30 (line 1057, cs.pos_age_min=best_age line 1067) but is persisted with ts=now, discarding best_age. _rescue_from_last_pos then computes `age = (now_utc - _lp_entry_ts(entry))/60` and accepts while `age < LAST_KNOWN_POS_TTL_MIN` (25min, line 213). So a delivery observed ~28min ago, saved at T, can be rescued up to 24min later: reported pos_age ~24min, true position age ~52min.
impact: A free (empty-bag) courier can be placed at a ~50-min-old last-delivery location while pos_age_min reports <=25min, mildly understating distance/ETA staleness. Bounded by design and arguably better than the BIALYSTOK_CENTER fiction it replaced; equal-treatment (LIVE) removes no_gps penalties so blast radius is small. In-lane 'stale pos' class.
protocol_note: last-known-pos store ts = SAVE time (tick), not the GPS/event observation time. TTL (25min) therefore bounds time-since-last-tick-save, not true position age; effective staleness can stack source-age (up to ~30min) on top of TTL. Anyone tightening freshness must subtract observation age at save (ts = now - cs.pos_age_min) or shorten TTL.
recommendation: Verify with courier_last_pos.json sample whether non-gps sources are persisted with ts=now; if so, consider storing ts adjusted by cs.pos_age_min (observation time) so TTL bounds true staleness. Read-only verification first; any change via ziomek-change-protocol (touches the FIX-2026-06-08 store + FAIL-12 Z-06 consumer).

verification.reasoning:
Mechanism CONFIRMED, impact OVERSTATED. The save block (courier_resolver.py:1223-1226) writes ts=_now_iso for every fleet member whose pos_source is in _LAST_POS_GOOD_SOURCES, discarding the real observation age. That frozenset (lines 130-134) DOES include the step-3 recent-activity sources last_delivered and last_picked_up_recent, accepted at best_age up to RECENT_MAX_MIN=30 (lines 1030/1057), and freshly computed so pos_from_store=False, so the save guard at line 1216 does NOT skip them. _rescue_from_last_pos (lines 201-220) derives age from the store ts (=save time) and accepts while age < LAST_KNOWN_POS_TTL_MIN=25, returning that age as cs.pos_age_min (line 1079). Worst case: a delivery observed ~30min ago is rescued ~24min later with reported pos_age ~24min while true position age ~54min. The code path and arithmetic are exactly as claimed.

However the claimed IMPACT (understated staleness skewing distance/ETA decisions) is latent, not live. (1) The only consumer that turns pos_age_min into a scoring penalty, ENABLE_GPS_AGE_DISCOUNT, is false in flags.json:170 with no systemd drop-in and no dispatch-shadow Environment= flipping it ON; its own common.py:2824-2833 note marks thresholds uncalibrated/deferred, so it is inert. title: GPS-02 teleport anchor uses store SAVE-time (tick) not GPS fix-time → shadow-calibrated implied_speed/jump skewed before any future flip
evidence: _gps_quality_anchor computes `anchor_age_min = (now_utc - _lp_entry_ts(store_entry))/60` (line 256) where the store ts is the previous tick's SAVE time, and `dt_seconds = (anchor_age_min - new_age_min)*60` (line 260). new_age_min is the REAL current fix age, so dt mixes tick-time (anchor) with fix-time (new). assess_teleport then gates `if dt/60 > anchor_max_age_min (8.0)` on this inter-quantity dt and computes `speed = jump/(dt/3600)`. The anchor coords actually correspond to the previous fix (up to GPS_FRESHNESS_MIN=5min old at save), so dt underestimates true elapsed → implied_speed_kmh overstated for borderline pairs.
impact: ENABLE_GPS_ACCURACY_TELEPORT_FILTER is OFF (missing in flags.json → code default False) so NO live decision impact today. But ENABLE_GPS_QUALITY_SHADOW (default True) is actively logging gps_quality_shadow.jsonl as the calibration corpus for the planned GPS-02 flip; skewed implied_speed/jump risks mis-setting GPS_TELEPORT_MAX_SPEED_KMH/MIN_JUMP_KM at flip time.
protocol_note: Before flipping ENABLE_GPS_ACCURACY_TELEPORT_FILTER, fix the anchor timing: anchor dt should be (anchor_fix_time → new_fix_time), but the store keeps only save-time ts. Calibration thresholds derived from current shadow log may be biased toward over-detecting teleports.
recommendation: When GPS-02 calibration resumes, re-derive thresholds only after either storing the GPS fix timestamp in the anchor or correcting dt by the anchor's fix-age. Verify against gps_quality_shadow.jsonl implied_speed distribution. Read-only; flip/threshold change via protocol.

The structural asymmetry is REAL and correctly located. panel_watcher.py:1424-1444 (disappeared-from-HTML, status 8/9) emits ORDER_RETURNED_TO_POOL + update_from_event but never calls _remove_stops_on_return, whereas the reconcile twin at 1828-1841 does. _remove_stops_on_return (667-690) is the sole caller of plan_manager.remove_stops, and its docstring claims SYMETRIA z assign/deliver/pickup (P-5 audyt 2026-06-24) -- so the disappeared path was skipped by that symmetry pass (genuine partial-twin, anti-pattern #2). update_from_event keeps the order with terminal status returned_to_pool (state_machine.py:798-805); delivered is pruned by advance_plan (panel_watcher.py:651-655), so only the disappeared-cancel lacks a pruner.

BUT the IMPACT is overstated, hence partial not confirmed. The claim leans on recanon subset perpetuation and 'full regen only for missing/partial, not supersets'. That ignores the live self-heal: gap_fill runs under ENABLE_PLAN_SEQUENCE_LOCK=1 (LIVE via dispatch-plan-recheck.service.d/unified-route-f1-f2.conf). After a cancel the active bag oids EXCLUDES the cancelled order, so _bag_signature(oids) no longer matches the plan's stored bag_signature -> MISMATCH -> _gap_fill_plans does NOT retime, it falls through to _gen_one_bag_plan(cid, active-oids) = full regen WITHOUT the cancelled stop (plan_recheck.py:1877-1893). _retime_one_bag_plan PRESERVES the stale bag_signature (line 1571), so even an interim RECANON_ON_WRITE retime cannot mask the mismatch. dispatch-plan-recheck.timer fires every ~5 min (verified live, last tick 11:07), so for any courier with >=1 remaining active order the ghost clears at the next tick. I also refuted my own counter-theory: AUTO_INVALIDATE_STALE is NOT in the plan-recheck service env (code default '0', not in drop-ins, live summary shows auto_invalidated:0), so the _check_plan terminal->invalidate route (plan_recheck.py:241-245 + run_recheck 2030-2032) is inert and does NOT clear ghosts -- the clearing is purely the LOCK signature-regen above.

Genuine residual (why not fully refuted, why P3 not none): if the cancelled order was the courier's ONLY active order, the courier drops out of the bags dict (built from ACTIVE_STATUSES only, plan_recheck.py:1866-1872), so gap_fill never processes it and the lone cancelled stop is not regenerated away; with AUTO_INVALIDATE off it persists. But whether a zero-active-order courier even renders a visible route ghost in console/app is unproven (build_view full-coverage path not opened). title: closed_ids (DOM) and order_ids (JS) are independent sources; disappeared path pre-empts the recanon-capable reconcile path
evidence: order_ids come from JS re.findall(r'id:\s*(\d{5,7})', html_clean) (panel_html_parser:87); closed_ids come from DOM blocks id="zamowienie_(\d+)" lacking data-idkurier scanned over raw html (157-163). They are NOT guaranteed subsets. In _diff_and_emit the disappeared loop (zid not in html_order_ids, JS-based, line 1387) runs BEFORE the reconcile loop (zid in closed, DOM-based, line 1778). When a cancelled order leaves the JS array, the disappeared path terminalizes state to returned_to_pool first; the reconcile loop then skips it (status no longer assigned/picked_up at 1776) — so the recanon-capable path at 1840 is bypassed. This makes pis-cancel-disappeared-no-recanon the COMMON path for such cancels, not a rare race.
impact: Amplifies the missing-recanon bug: for cancels that drop out of the JS id array (typical), the only path that fires is the one missing remove_stops/recanon.
protocol_note: closed_ids and order_ids derive from different HTML sources (DOM vs JS) and the two terminalization loops race within one tick (disappeared first). Do not assume closed_ids ⊆ order_ids when reasoning about which loop handles a terminal transition.
recommendation: When fixing pis-cancel-disappeared-no-recanon, do not rely on reconcile to catch the same order — it is pre-empted. Optionally unify ORDER_RETURNED_TO_POOL handling into one helper used by both loops.

verification.reasoning:
The finding's code-level facts are correct, but its operational claim -- that the disappeared path pre-empts reconcile and is the COMMON path for cancels -- is refuted.

Confirmed sub-facts I re-read: (a) the disappeared loop runs first; for status_id in (8,9) it emits ORDER_RETURNED_TO_POOL + update_from_event but has NO _remove_stops_on_return/recanon (panel_watcher.py:1424-1448). (b) the reconcile loop (1773-1844) for sid in (8,9) DOES call _remove_stops_on_return at 1840, which internally runs plan_manager.remove_stops + plan_recheck.recanon_courier(reason=return) (667-692). (c) the terminal-skip guard at 1382-1384 means once disappeared sets returned_to_pool, reconcile skips at 1776. So a real recanon divergence between the two paths exists -- but that is the SEPARATE finding pis-cancel-disappeared-no-recanon, not this amplification claim.

What collapses the common-path claim: closed_ids is DEFINITIONALLY a DOM block. To land in `closed` (panel_html_parser.py:157-163) an order must STILL be rendered as id="zamowienie_X" without data-idkurier -- i.e. a cancelled order caught by reconcile is still present in the DOM. The DOM source (id="zamowienie_X") and the JS source (id: X, line 87) are designed consistent: the parser carries a cross-source guard (panel_html_parser.py:171-192) that WARNS only when |DOM-JS| drift exceeds 5%, treating divergence as a rare anomaly, and the comment at line 88 states the JS id is "consistent z id=zamowienie_X HTML attr". Therefore in a normal tick an order present in `closed` (DOM) is also present in `html_order_ids` (JS), so the disappeared guard `zid not in html_order_ids` (1387) does NOT fire for it -- the reconcile (recanon-capable) path is what runs.

For the finding's pre-emption to occur, a single order must be DOM-present-but-JS-absent within the SAME tick (in `closed` yet not in html_order_ids) -- precisely the per-order divergence the cross-source guard flags as anomalous/rare. The finding offers no evidence cancelled orders systematically drop from the JS array before the DOM. By timing, the reconcile path actually catches the cancel while it is still a closed DOM block (recanon fires), and only after full panel removal does state go terminal -- the opposite of the claim. The "NOT guaranteed subsets" statement is literally true at the regex level but does not establish that the disappeared path is the typical cancel path. Hence the amplification/common-path claim is refuted; the genuine residual gap (a cancel that vanishes from BOTH DOM and JS in one tick lacking recanon) is a rare edge handled by the separate finding, not this one.

When the console path at :1105 succeeds it sets _console_done=True (:1120), so :1146 is unreachable and the flag is a no-op on the primary live path. That part holds.

Two things break the P2 claim. (1) NOT dead code: _console_done is True only if the console try-block succeeds; if route_podjazdy import fails (_route_podjazdy=None at :40) or order_podjazdy raises (except at :1121-1122), _console_done stays False and the ziomek_plan branch with the flag executes. It is a live fail-soft fallback consumer. (2) The flag intent (carried-first relax, app order = console order) is achieved on the live path regardless: the console path never calls _prioritize_carried_dropoffs / _reorder_pickup_steps_by_committed (those calls exist only inside the bypassed ziomek_plan block at :1148/:1154), and the app routes through the SAME dispatch_v2.route_podjazdy module the console uses (APP_ROUTE_FROM_CONSOLE=1). So app-vs-console parity is genuinely ON and the drop-in's stated effect is delivered structurally. Hence the claims 'false rollback lever / root mechanism of app-console divergence / misleads operators into believing parity is configured ON' are refuted: parity is real, no divergence is caused. What remains is a genuine partial-change smell, a redundant control lever that does nothing on the primary live config (audit lens #1/#9), but harmless and still functional on fallback. That merits P3 housekeeping, not P2.

title: Console fleet_state is a hand-synced PARALLEL re-implementation of route_podjazdy, not a delegation as the module docstring claims
evidence: route_podjazdy.py docstring lines 3-6 claim it is imported by 'konsolę koordynatora (panel fleet_state._build_route -> deleguje tutaj)'. But fleet_state.py does NOT import route_podjazdy (grep: only a comment mention at line 257 'Lustro route_podjazdy._plan_pickup_clusters'); it contains its OWN copies _plan_pickup_clusters (254-279), _pickup_runs (282-316) and inline plan_drop_rank (365-378). The two copies are byte-for-byte parallel for the fallback path but must be kept in sync by hand.
impact: Two independent implementations of pickup-run / cluster / drop-rank logic that can silently drift. The app already grew a divergent feature (raw-canon relax) only in one copy (see cap-carried-relax-app-console-divergence). The docstring's 'deleguje tutaj' is false and will mislead future maintainers into assuming a single source of truth.
protocol_note: Console and app ordering live in TWO files, not one. ziomek_time_route_monitor.py is the only thing enforcing their equivalence. Any change to pickup grouping / cluster logic must be mirrored in BOTH fleet_state.py and route_podjazdy.py and verified by the monitor.
recommendation: Treat fleet_state ordering helpers and route_podjazdy as a twin pair in the completeness map; keep ziomek_time_route_monitor green. Correct the route_podjazdy docstring claim.

verification.reasoning: Tried to refute but the code confirms the core claim. route_podjazdy.py lines 1-18 docstring line 4 asserts the coordinator console (fleet_state._build_route) 'deleguje tutaj'. Reality: fleet_state.py imports (lines 14-27) contain NO import of route_podjazdy; the only reference is a comment at line 257 ('Lustro route_podjazdy._plan_pickup_clusters'). fleet_state owns parallel copies _plan_pickup_clusters (254-279), _pickup_runs (282-316), inline plan_drop_rank (365-378). A diff of _plan_pickup_clusters across the two files shows identical control flow but divergent docstrings/comments and visibility (route_podjazdy exposes public pickup_runs/plan_drop_rank; fleet_state uses private _pickup_runs + inline rank). So 'deleguje tutaj' is false. Stronger than claimed: route_podjazdy.py is imported by NO production module — grep -rln in dispatch_v2 returns only tests/test_route_podjazdy_plan_aware.py and an eod_drafts verify script; courier_api/ has zero references, so even the second claimed consumer (courier_orders, the app) carries its own rendering. The single-source-of-truth module is effectively unwired; both real surfaces hand-sync mirrors. The one inaccuracy is 'byte-for-byte parallel' — they are mirror-logic, not byte-identical (docstrings/comments differ) — but the finding's substantive thesis (hand-synced duplication that can drift, false delegation docstring) holds and is self-documented by fleet_state's own line-257 'Lustro' comment. No runtime/decision bug, pure maintainability + misleading-doc debt, so P3 is correct.

title: Parity monitor's console side renders raw-canon despite loading only flags.systemd.env (which lacks PANEL_FLAG_TRUST_CANON_ORDER) — env source unexplained, latent false-parity risk
evidence: The monitor unit loads only `EnvironmentFile=-.../flags.systemd.env`, and that file has NO PANEL_FLAG_TRUST_CANON_ORDER (grep shows only PLAN_AWARE_PODJAZDY/SKIP_INVALIDATED_PLAN/DROP_GEOMETRY). DEFAULT_FLAGS['TRUST_CANON_ORDER']=False (flags.py:28). Yet the monitor's console output (route_console -> _build_route -> flag('TRUST_CANON_ORDER')) shows carried-NOT-first (raw canon) for cid 509 — which only _order_from_plan_seq can produce — so flag('TRUST_CANON_ORDER') is effectively True in that process. The monitor explicitly mirrors PLAN_AWARE by reading the courier-api drop-in file directly (_plan_aware_flag_on, lines 128-137) but does NOT do the same for TRUST_CANON_ORDER; it relies on ambient env it does not declare.
impact: Currently the monitor DOES reflect production (panel has TRUST_CANON_ORDER=1) and is catching the divergence, so this is not an active failure. But the monitor's correctness depends on an env value it neither loads from the panel drop-in nor mirrors explicitly. If flags.systemd.env / panel drop-in / ambient env drift, the monitor could silently flip its console side to the fallback path and report false parity (0 mismatches) while production still diverges.
protocol_note: ziomek_time_route_monitor only mirrors PLAN_AWARE and APP_ROUTE flags explicitly; TRUST_CANON_ORDER is implicit. To trust its GO/NO-GO, confirm the monitor process actually has PANEL_FLAG_TRUST_CANON_ORDER matching nadajesz-panel before relying on a 0-mismatch reading.
recommendation: Next session: dump the monitor process env during a daytime tick (read /proc of its transient pid or add an explicit mirror like _plan_aware_flag_on for TRUST_CANON_ORDER) to confirm it equals the panel's. Do not treat monitor 0-mismatch as proof of parity until this is pinned.

verification.reasoning: The finding claims the monitor's TRUST_CANON_ORDER env source is "unexplained / ambient / not declared," creating a latent false-parity risk. This is factually wrong: the author inspected only the main .service file's EnvironmentFile line and missed the systemd drop-in directory. /etc/systemd/system/ziomek-time-route-monitor.service.d/trust-canon.conf (created 2026-06-24) explicitly declares Environment=PANEL_FLAG_TRUST_CANON_ORDER=1, with a comment stating its purpose: render the console side via the production path (nadajesz-panel runs PANEL_FLAG_TRUST_CANON_ORDER=1), otherwise the monitor would take the fallback. flags.py:119-124 confirms flag('TRUST_CANON_ORDER') reads os.getenv('PANEL_FLAG_TRUST_CANON_ORDER') and '1' maps to True, so the monitor process has the flag effectively ON via its own pinned drop-in. The env is fully explained and explicitly declared as persistent systemd config, not ambient; flags.systemd.env lacking the key is irrelevant because the drop-in sets it unconditionally on every tick. The author also misread _plan_aware_flag_on (lines 126-137) as proof TRUST_CANON is 'not mirrored'; in fact PLAN_AWARE is mirrored at runtime (reads courier-api's drop-in, whose value can vary) while TRUST_CANON is hard-pinned in the monitor's own drop-in to match the panel — both faithful, neither ambient. The latent-drift premise collapses. No genuine bug or knowledge gap; the prior auditor simply did not read the .service.d/ directory.

title: ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY does not actually make LGBM primary — flip is a behavioral no-op (logging-only)
evidence: predict_two_model_for_decision gates on `flag('ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY', False) or flag('ENABLE_LGBM_TWOMODEL_SHADOW', False)` and returns a TwoModelResult. In dispatch_pipeline the result is only written to top[0].metrics['lgbm_twomodel_shadow'] and logged — never used to reorder top/feasible. grep of ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY across non-test code shows ONLY: common.py def, the flag-gate in predict_two_model_for_decision, and comments. The docstring itself says 'różnica semantyczna pojawi się dopiero gdy selekcja zacznie konsumować winner_cid' — i.e. the selection consumer is NOT built. So flipping ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY=ON yields identical behavior to ENABLE_LGBM_TWOMODEL_SHADOW (shadow compute only), NOT LGBM-driven picks.
impact: flags.json comment 'FLIP primary = osobna flaga ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY + ACK' implies this flag is the lever to make LGBM primary. A future session could flip it expecting LGBM to drive assignments, get zero effect and no error, and falsely conclude LGBM-primary was deployed. The actual arbitration (solo↔bundle, documented unresolved in ml_inference.py:779-791) and the selection-consumer code do not exist.
protocol_note: ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY is a pre-declared flag with NO selection consumer. Do NOT flip it expecting LGBM to become the decision-maker — it only triggers the same shadow computation as TWOMODEL_SHADOW. Making LGBM primary requires building winner_cid consumption into selection AND resolving the solo/bundle arbitration blocker (ml_inference.py:779-791).
recommendation: Next session: before any 'LGBM primary' work, confirm there is still no code path consuming TwoModelResult.winner_cid for reordering; treat ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY as documentation-only until a consumer + solo/bundle arbiter are implemented and replay-proven.

verification.reasoning: The mechanism is confirmed and even understated. predict_two_model_for_decision (ml_inference.py:849-851) returns a result if ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY OR ENABLE_LGBM_TWOMODEL_SHADOW is set, but the ONLY pipeline call site (dispatch_pipeline.py:6202) gates exclusively on C.flag('ENABLE_LGBM_TWOMODEL_SHADOW'), not on ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY. The TwoModelResult is written solely to top[0].metrics['lgbm_twomodel_shadow'] and logged (6219-6230); it never reorders top/feasible, and winner_cid only feeds an agreement_with_primary metric. grep of ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY in non-test code yields only the common.py:2217 def, the OR-gate, and comments — no selection consumer exists. The docstring (ml_inference.py:838-846) and §OGRANICZENIE (784-791) explicitly state both modes are logging-only and solo<->bundle arbitration is the unresolved blocker. So ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY=ON does NOT make LGBM primary. It is in fact more inert than the report says: with TWOMODEL_SHADOW off, flipping PRIMARY alone yields zero compute (no call site reaches it), not even shadow compute. Severity downgraded P2->P3 because the dead-flag is loudly self-documented in three places as not-yet-consumed, carries no silent-wrong-pick or destructive risk, and the only residual issue is the mildly misleading flags.json comment 'FLIP primary = osobna flaga ENABLE_LGBM_PRIMARY + ACK'.

title: prep-bias R6 anchor shift applied only in feasibility_v2, not in route_simulator/plan_recheck/_count_sla_violations
evidence: feasibility_v2.py:1063-1069 applies `anchor = anchor + timedelta(minutes=_shift_min)` from prep_bias_anchor.anchor_shift_min only inside the feasibility R6 gate, behind ENABLE_PREP_BIAS_TABLE. grep shows prep_bias_anchor is imported ONLY by feasibility_v2.py. The comment at feasibility_v2.py:1044-1045 explicitly states the R6 thermal anchor is otherwise '1:1 z route_simulator _compute_per_order_delivery_minutes' and that prep_bias is layered on AFTER, only here ('gate-stricter'). route_simulator's predicted_delivered_at and _count_sla_violations use the UN-shifted anchor.
impact: On flip of ENABLE_PREP_BIAS_TABLE, the feasibility R6 gate becomes stricter (anchor earlier → bag_time larger) while the plan's displayed predicted_delivered_at and the plan-side SLA violation count keep the un-shifted anchor → gate-vs-plan/display R6 divergence. This is the same class as the known open O2 ready-anchor gap (_count_sla_violations + feasibility ~1156). Currently latent because the flag is OFF.
protocol_note: Any flip of ENABLE_PREP_BIAS_TABLE must mirror the anchor shift into route_simulator _compute_per_order_delivery_minutes and the plan-side SLA violation counter (and plan_recheck phase-B), or the feasibility gate and the displayed/plan R6 will disagree (O2 class).
recommendation: Before flipping prep-bias to main, map all R6 anchor computation sites and apply the same anchor_shift_min; add a parity test gate-vs-plan bag_time. The flip-evidence monitor (prep_bias_shadow_monitor → prep_bias_decision_time_replay) already reads the correct prep_bias_table.json, so the evidence path is sound — the gap is only the live twin-path wiring.

verification.reasoning:
The finding's raw fact is accurate but its bug framing is wrong: the behavior is documented intentional design, not a twin-path defect.

(1) feasibility_v2.py:1063-1085 applies the prep_bias shift ONLY inside the R6 gate, behind C.flag('ENABLE_PREP_BIAS_TABLE', False), only for declared anchors (anchor_src pickup_ready_at/tsp_pickup_at). The [C2] comment states it moves the anchor earlier so R6 fires earlier (freshness protection, NEVER more liberal; negative bias clamped to 0 in prep_bias_anchor.anchor_shift_min). So it can only ever make the HARD gate stricter.

(2) The decisive evidence is route_simulator_v2.py:663-669, r6_thermal_anchor's docstring: 'prep_bias (feasibility-only, gate-stricter) NAKLADA wolajacy PO — to swiadoma asymetria (bramka ostrzejsza), nie dryft kotwicy bazowej' = a CONSCIOUS asymmetry (stricter gate), explicitly NOT base-anchor drift. The baseline thermal anchor is shared 1:1 between feasibility and route_simulator (INV-R6-ANCHOR-CONSISTENCY). prep_bias is an additive conservatism on an ALREADY-consistent baseline, which the finding itself concedes is '1:1'.

(3) This is NOT the same class as the O2 ready-anchor gap. O2 is a genuine BASELINE inconsistency (feasibility ready-anchor vs _count_sla_violations using picked_up/now). prep_bias is deliberate extra HARD-gate margin on a consistent baseline — the desired HARD-stricter-than-SOFT direction. HARD-before-SOFT (_assert_feasibility_first) means if feasibility rejects, the soft _count_sla_violations metric is moot for that candidate.

(4) The 'display divergence' framing is unfounded: predicted_delivered_at (the displayed ETA) is computed by the simulator from OSRM travel times, NOT anchor-derived. The anchor only feeds bag_time = pred - anchor inside the gate; shifting it never changes the displayed pred. There is no display surface for bag_time/anchor.

(5) Flag state: ENABLE_PREP_BIAS_TABLE = false in /root/.openclaw/workspace/scripts/flags.json; decision_flag reads flags.json -> module const -> False; no systemd drop-in forces it ON (only dispatch-prep-bias-shadow-monitor.service, read-only, whose own Description notes 'flag ENABLE_PREP_BIAS_TABLE OFF'). So latent today, and even on flip the result is the intended gate-stricter asymmetry, not a divergence bug.

Minor note: the finding's grep claim ('prep_bias_anchor imported ONLY by feasibility_v2') is true for that module, but the prep_bias CONCEPT also lives at dispatch_pipeline.py:2037 (_sync_effective_ready via calib_maps.prep_bias_for) for syncworka spread, also gated by the same flag — the finding missed this second consumer. Net: factually the twin-path observation holds, but it describes intentional, documented HARD-gate conservatism, so it does not merit a tech-debt severity.

title: Two divergent prep-bias artifacts: shadow_dispatcher telemetry uses different table/stat/sign than the main R6 correction
evidence: shadow_dispatcher.py:506-513 computes prep_bias_min/effective_ready_shadow via calib_maps.prep_bias_for → reads dispatch_state/restaurant_prep_bias.json, key 'bias_med' PER SLOT, ready+bias (later), no clamp/cap (mtime Jun 27, 41KB, actively regenerated). The MAIN R6 correction (feasibility) uses prep_bias_anchor.anchor_shift_min → reads dispatch_state/prep_bias_table.json, key 'bias_p80_min' GLOBAL/no-slot, anchor−bias (earlier), positive-only clamp, 20-min cap (mtime Jun 20, 7KB). Different files, statistic, slot-granularity, sign convention.
impact: shadow_decisions.jsonl carries prep_bias_min/effective_ready_shadow that does NOT represent the magnitude/direction the live R6 correction would apply. Anyone reading that field as 'the R6 prep-bias' would be misled. The actual flip-decision evidence path (prep_bias_shadow_monitor) correctly uses prep_bias_table.json, so this is confusion/coupling, not a flip-blocker.
protocol_note: There are TWO prep-bias tables. restaurant_prep_bias.json (bias_med, per-slot) drives shadow_dispatcher's effective_ready_shadow telemetry and ETA quantile maps; prep_bias_table.json (bias_p80, global) drives the R6 anchor correction. Do not conflate them.
recommendation: Document the split or unify the source; ensure any prep-bias GO/NO-GO uses prep_bias_table.json (it currently does via prep_bias_decision_time_replay). Treat shadow_dispatcher prep_bias_min as a separate ETA-readiness telemetry, not the R6 lever.

verification.reasoning: Facts CONFIRMED, impact overstated, design intentional -> P3 nitpick not a defect. shadow_dispatcher.py:498-517: effective_ready_shadow = pickup_ready_at + calib_maps.prep_bias_for (restaurant_prep_bias.json, key bias_med, PER-SLOT, ready+bias forward, no clamp; file Jun28 41KB). prep_bias_anchor.py:33,108-130: prep_bias_table.json, bias_p80_min, GLOBAL, anchor-earlier shift -min(raw,20), positive-only clamp, 20min cap (file Jun20 7KB). So different file/stat/granularity/sign all true. BUT: (1) shadow_dispatcher.py:499-503 explicitly comments the field is pure order-level telemetry that does NOT affect decisions and R6 consumption is a separate flag. (2) ENABLE_PREP_BIAS_TABLE=false in flags.json:161 and feasibility_v2.py:1064 gates anchor_shift_min behind it, so the main R6 correction is NOT live -> no live correction to be confused with. (3) The artifacts SHOULD diverge by purpose: R6 anchor uses conservative p80+positive-only clamp (never relax R6), telemetry/ETA path wants median; the sign difference is just two reference frames (ready-forward vs anchor-backward) for the same kitchen-slower direction, not a contradiction. (4) prep_bias_shadow_monitor.py:35,91 (flip evidence) correctly uses the table/anchor path. Net: documented, non-live, by-design coupling note; not a behavioral bug or flip-blocker.

title: validation_gate_lgbm.py gates the CANCELLED LGBM-PRIMARY deploy with unreachable GO criteria and no live consumer

The gate is a manual CLI with no timer/automation consuming its verdict.

title: LGBM pool tier-count features depend on courier_name→tier name match; silent 'unknown' degradation possible
evidence: _compute_all_candidate_features builds gold_in_pool/std_plus_in_pool/... from tier_counts keyed by self._name_to_tier.get(name) where name = _get_candidate_attr(c,'name')/courier_name; METRICS_MAP (ml_inference.py:126-131) has no 'name'/'courier_name' key, so it falls through to getattr(c,'name',None). _name_to_tier is built from courier_tiers.json bag.tier keyed by the courier display NAME (L181-190). verification.reasoning: The file diff is real and confirmed: the repo override.conf is a frozen pre-ETAP4 snapshot (git log shows a single commit 5d4f116 'snapshot', never updated) carrying 6 extra Environment= lines absent from the installed /etc copy (mtime Jun 11, ETAP4 header documenting migration to flags.json). To that extent the finding stands. But the claimed IMPACT collapses on verification: (1) decision_flag (common.py:283) resolves load_flags().get(name, globals().get(name,False)) -- flags.json is read FIRST and env is never consulted for the migrated booleans; all 5 booleans are true in flags.json, so re-applying the repo file changes nothing and creates no env-over-flags.json double-source for them. (2) OBJ_SPAN_COST_COEFF is NOT a flags.json key (grep exit 1); it is a pure env-default const (common.py:2510, default 1.0), so the repo value 1.0 equals the live module default and there is NO double-source to violate -- the finding's claim 'all 6 present in flags.json' and 'double-source for OBJ_SPAN_COST_COEFF' are factually wrong. (3) The 'documented apply path (cp systemd/*.conf to /etc)' that would cause silent divergence is not found; every deploy doc edits /etc in place with a .bak, never cp from the repo systemd tree, so the divergence risk is hypothetical. Net: a benign stale reference snapshot with zero behavioral consequence even if redeployed; merits at most low housekeeping P3, not a single-source/decision-impact violation.

The mechanics are accurately described (flag IS live: flags.json:217 true + memory confirms #483000 LIVE; promotion at 6105-6134 runs AFTER _assert_feasibility_first at 5794; it sets a verdict==NO candidate's feasibility_verdict to MAYBE and inserts at top[0]). 1) It STRICTLY IMPROVES HARD-R6 rather than weakening it. _feas_carry_readmit_pick (1295-1356) fires ONLY when the live winner top[0] is itself a forgiven R6-breacher: chosen_objm = objm(chosen,'objm_r6_breach_max_min'); if chosen_objm is None or <= 0: return None (1310-1312). Opposite of weakening HARD.

2) The 'verdict-set raises -> swallowed -> NO stays at top[0]' path is unreachable. Candidate (2564-2578) is a plain @dataclass (no __slots__, not frozen; feasibility_verdict is a normal str field). Attribute assignment cannot raise AttributeError; the except Exception: pass (6117-6119) is defensive dead-catch, not a live hole.

Net: factually-true structural observation (NO->MAYBE mutation after the guard, guard cannot re-run) but mis-classified as a HARD-weakening risk. Both POST_SHIFT (not in flags.json → module default '0' = OFF) and OBJM_LEXR6_SELECT_SHADOW (false) are OFF, so this is doubly inert. _best_effort_objm_pick / _feas_carry_readmit_pick / _objm_lexr6_d2_pick all use the module (post-shift-aware) lex_qual, so the LIVE paths are mutually consistent.
impact: Zero today. If both POST_SHIFT and the lexr6 shadow are ever ON together, the shadow's lex_qual would diverge from live by ignoring the leading post-shift term, compounding the bucket divergence already noted in twin-objm-lexr6-shadow-stale-bucket-and-lexqual.
recommendation: Folded into the objm-lexr6-unify completion (same fix as twin-objm-lexr6-shadow-stale-bucket-and-lexqual). No separate action.

verification.reasoning: All code facts in the finding check out and I could not refute the divergence. (1) Module canon objm_lexr6.lex_qual IS post-shift-aware: objm_lexr6.py:40-46 builds 3-tuple base=(r6, late_pickup_committed_max, new_pickup_late_min), then `if C.decision_flag('ENABLE_POST_SHIFT_OVERRUN_PENALTY')` returns ((post_shift_overrun_penalty,))+base = 4-tuple. (2) Inline _objm_lexr6_shadow._lex_qual at dispatch_pipeline.py:1128-1132 is a hardcoded 3-tuple with NO post-shift leading term = real twin-path divergence (anti-pattern #2/#4). (3) Both flags OFF in-process, doubly inert: ENABLE_OBJM_LEXR6_SELECT_SHADOW=false in flags.json:191 with no env/drop-in override (recursive grep of /etc/systemd/system found none), and the shadow only runs when this flag is ON (gate at 6088-6089), so the inline copy is not even executed today; ENABLE_POST_SHIFT_OVERRUN_PENALTY is module-default '0' (common.py:1800-1801), absent from flags.json, no env/drop-in override. (4) Live selection paths use the canon module (post-shift-aware) as claimed: _best_effort_objm_pick -> _OL.lex_qual (665/667), _feas_carry_readmit_pick -> _OL.lex_qual (1313/1345/1346), _objm_lexr6_d2_pick -> objm_lexr6.pick (1372). The divergent inline copy is confined to the observational shadow. Severity stays P3 (not higher): _objm_lexr6_shadow is purely OBSERVATIONAL per its docstring (1101-1102: writes only top[0].metrics objm_lexr6_*, never mutates top/feasible/verdict), so even in the never-yet-reached both-ON worst case it would only corrupt a shadow-logged validation metric, never a live verdict/feasibility/committed-time. Docstring at 1106-1108 explicitly flags the inline copy as deferred-unification debt (re-point shadow to module after at#152 PASS), confirming this is known partial-change debt, not a live bug. title: Reassignment fires recanon/invalidate only for the gaining courier; the losing courier's plan retains the reassigned stop with no immediate maintenance
evidence: In the reassign branch (panel_watcher.py:1486-1512) on `REASSIGNED {zid} {state_courier} -> {panel_courier}` only `_save_plan_on_assign_signal(zid, panel_courier)` runs — recanon/invalidate/redecide all target the NEW courier B. Nothing references `state_courier` (old A): no `_remove_stops_on_return(state_courier, zid)`, no recanon, no invalidate for A. state_machine COURIER_ASSIGNED just sets orders_state[oid].courier_id=B (state_machine.py:520-602), so A's bag shrinks but A's courier_plans.json plan still holds the reassigned order's dropoff stop. Contrast: deliver=advance_plan+recanon (632-664), cancel/return=remove_stops+recanon (667-692), pickup=mark_picked_up+recanon (695-726) — every other bag-mutating transition immediately maintains the affected courier's plan. Note recanon alone could NOT fix A even if called: recanon_courier loads plan WITHOUT active_bag_oids (plan_recheck.py:1782) and _retime_one_bag_plan/_apply_canon_order_invariants operate on plan['stops'] (not filtered to oids) — pruning requires remove_stops first, which is exactly the return-handler pattern.
impact: After a coordinator reassign, the losing courier's saved plan carries a phantom dropoff for an order they no longer have until the next 5-min plan_recheck tick regenerates it (bag_signature mismatch → _gen_one_bag_plan). Decision/scoring engine is shielded because dispatch_pipeline loads with load_plan(active_bag_oids) (dispatch_pipeline.py:2365,3630) which invalidates-on-read when a dropoff is outside the live bag (plan_manager.py:138-143). But the coordinator console reads courier_plans.json directly (fleet_state TRUST_CANON_ORDER) without that guard, so the old courier's rendered route can show a phantom delivery stop and skewed ETAs for up to ~5 min.
twin_paths: panel_watcher.py:_remove_stops_on_return (the symmetric handler the reassign-loser should reuse for old courier)
protocol_note: The recanon completeness map is '4 handlers assign/deliver/pickup/cancel'. There is a 5th bag-mutating transition — the REASSIGN LOSER — not in that map. Any future recanon/plan-maintenance change must treat reassign as TWO mutations: gainer (B, already handled) and loser (A, currently unhandled = should mirror _remove_stops_on_return + recanon for old courier).
recommendation: Verify whether the console fleet_state filters plan stops by the courier's current gastro bag before rendering (if it does, harm is nil and this is cosmetic-internal only). If not, next session should add to the reassign branch a remove_stops(old_state_courier, zid)+recanon(old_state_courier) mirroring _remove_stops_on_return, via the full ziomek-change-protocol (twin paths: initial-assign at 1372, panel_diff at 1480, packs_fallback at 1624, cold_start at 2234 — check which carry a known old courier_id).

verification.reasoning: Mechanism is correct but the load-bearing impact is false. TRUE: the reassign branch (panel_watcher.py:1485-1514) only runs _save_plan_on_assign_signal(zid, panel_courier) for gaining courier B; no _remove_stops_on_return/recanon/invalidate targets state_courier A, so A's courier_plans.json keeps the phantom dropoff until next plan_recheck. REFUTED: the claimed impact (coordinator console rendering a phantom delivery stop / skewed ETAs for A for ~5 min) cannot occur because the console is bag-filtered exactly like the engine. fleet_state.read_fleet (fleet_state.py:694-717) builds each courier's bag ONLY from orders_state filtered by courier_id==cid and status in _ACTIVE_BAG. After COURIER_ASSIGNED (state_machine.py:519+) sets orders_state[zid].courier_id=B, zid leaves A's bag. _build_route (346,368) sets by_oid from that bag; _order_from_plan_seq (316-318) does b=by_oid.get(oid); if b is None: continue, dropping the phantom zid stop before route assembly. The TRUST_CANON coverage gate (401-406) uses need_drop built from the bag, which also excludes zid, so it still passes. The phantom never enters the rendered route and its plan_pred entry is never consumed by a surviving stop, so no ETA skew. All three consumers are shielded: engine via load_plan(active_bag_oids) (conceded by finding), console via bag-filter (proven here), app via orders_state. Residual is purely inert on-disk staleness for <=5 min with zero rendered effect; housekeeping, not the P2 console-render bug claimed.

title: Committed-time changes (CZAS_KURIERA_UPDATED / PICKUP_TIME_UPDATED) bump plan_version but never trigger immediate recanon, despite canon pickup-order being committed-sorted
evidence: On CZAS_KURIERA_UPDATED (panel_watcher.py:2085) and PICKUP_TIME_UPDATED (2119) the only plan action is `_invalidate_plan_on_committed_change(zid, courier_id)` which (559-602) just calls plan_manager.touch_plan() to bump plan_version for an app refetch — it does NOT call recanon_courier. Yet _apply_canon_order_invariants (plan_recheck.py:1468-1484) reorders pickups by `_ck` = czas_kuriera_warsaw ascending, so a committed-time change can change the correct canonical sequence. _bag_signature (327-338) encodes only (order_id, picked_up_bool), NOT committed time, so under ENABLE_PLAN_SEQUENCE_LOCK the 5-min tick takes the retime branch (plan_recheck.py:1878-1885) — which still re-applies canon invariants inside _retime_one_bag_plan (1553-1555), so the new committed order IS eventually reflected, just not immediately.
impact: A committed/ready-time edit that should re-order which pickup goes first is not re-sequenced at the moment of the edit (only plan_version bumped); the corrected order lands on the next plan_recheck tick (≤5 min). Asymmetric vs pickup/assign which recanon immediately. Bounded and self-correcting because the tick's retime path re-runs _apply_canon_order_invariants (both flags live in dispatch-plan-recheck unit).
protocol_note: Committed-time mutation is a canon-ordering input (pickups sorted by czas_kuriera) but is handled as a pure view-refresh, not a recanon trigger. If a future change tightens immediate committed-order enforcement, the committed-change handler is the missing recanon callsite. Note bag_signature deliberately excludes committed time — so PLAN_SEQUENCE_LOCK alone does not re-decide on committed change; it relies on the retime-path invariants.
recommendation: Measure-first: confirm via shadow whether the ≤5-min deferral causes any wrong-order pickup window before adding recanon to the committed-change handler — the existing tick retime+invariants may already make this a no-op (it re-applies committed sort). Do not add a recanon call without proving positive impact ON vs OFF.

verification.reasoning: Could not refute; every cited link verified against current code AND live flag state. (1) panel_watcher.py:2085 (CZAS_KURIERA_UPDATED) and :2119 (PICKUP_TIME_UPDATED) call only _invalidate_plan_on_committed_change, which (559-602) calls plan_manager.touch_plan() — bumps plan_version, no recanon; its own docstring confirms re-sequencing is deferred to next plan_recheck tick. (2) Asymmetry real: _save_plan_on_assign_signal (612-618) and deliver/return/pickup (662/690/723) all call recanon_courier immediately; committed change does not. (3) _apply_canon_order_invariants (1468-1481) sorts pickups by _ck=czas_kuriera_warsaw ascending — so a committed edit CAN change canonical pickup order. (4) _bag_signature (327-338) encodes only oid:picked_up_bool, ignoring committed time, so signature is unchanged on a committed edit. (5) Under ENABLE_PLAN_SEQUENCE_LOCK the unchanged signature takes the retime branch (1877-1885), and _retime_one_bag_plan re-applies _apply_canon_order_invariants (1553-1555), self-correcting on next tick. (6) BOTH flags are LIVE in the plan-recheck unit via drop-in unified-route-f1-f2.conf (ENABLE_PLAN_SEQUENCE_LOCK=1, ENABLE_PLAN_CANON_ORDER_INVARIANTS=1); they default 0 at module level and are NOT in flags.json — confirmed by reading the drop-in directly, not inferring from os.environ; the FLAG_FINGERPRINT omits them (curated subset). Net: genuine but bounded, self-correcting (<=5 min) sequencing-latency asymmetry with a defensible design rationale (lightweight touch path for committed edits; per-tick retime re-enforces committed-sorted canon regardless). P3 is fair; not higher because no HARD rule is violated and the corrected order always lands within one tick.

title: Tier-aware R6 HARD cap (hard_tier_bag_cap/would_hard_cap) and the courier tier driving it (cs_tier_bag/cs_tier_label) never reach shadow_decisions.jsonl
evidence: feasibility_v2.py:462-465: metrics["hard_tier_bag_cap"] = _hard_cap; metrics["would_hard_cap"] = bag_after > _hard_cap; if metrics["would_hard_cap"] and C.load_flags().get("ENABLE_HARD_TIER_BAG_CAP", False): return ("NO", f"hard_tier_bag_cap ({courier_tier or '?'} {bag_after}>{_hard_cap})", ...). dispatch_pipeline.py:5436-5437: "cs_tier_label": getattr(cs, "tier_label", None), "cs_tier_bag": getattr(cs, "tier_bag", None). grep "hard_tier_bag_cap"/"would_hard_cap"/"cs_tier_bag"/"cs_tier_label" in shadow_dispatcher.py = 0 each; none of these prefixes (hard_/would_/cs_) are in _AUTO_PROP_PREFIXES.
impact: The actual R6 HARD cap value (35 T1/2 vs 40 T3) and the tier that selected it are invisible offline. The domain invariant 'R6 cap is TIER-AWARE' cannot be audited from shadow_decisions: you cannot tell which cap a candidate was held to, nor pre-validate the ENABLE_HARD_TIER_BAG_CAP gate (would_hard_cap computed always, enforced only when flag on). r04 serializes a tier *suggestion*, not the tier actually used.
protocol_note: ETAP 4 of ziomek-change-protocol requires 'metryka w shadow_decisions.jsonl' as proof. would_hard_cap is computed-always specifically for shadow pre-flip validation but is dropped by the serializer, so the protocol's shadow-validation proof for ENABLE_HARD_TIER_BAG_CAP is currently impossible. Any future R6-cap change must add hard_tier_bag_cap/would_hard_cap/cs_tier_bag/cs_tier_label to BOTH serializer locations.
recommendation: Verify by reading feasibility_v2.py:455-465 + dispatch_pipeline.py:5430-5440; confirm no consumer reconstructs the cap. If touching R6/tier, add these 4 keys to LOCATION A and LOCATION B (or a 'r6_'/'tier_' prefix to _AUTO_PROP_PREFIXES) and assert flag ON!=OFF leaves a record difference.

verification.reasoning:
Mechanical claim CONFIRMED: hard_tier_bag_cap/would_hard_cap (feasibility_v2.py:462-464) and cs_tier_label/cs_tier_bag (dispatch_pipeline.py:5436-5437) are written into the metrics dict but never serialized. shadow_dispatcher.py _serialize_candidate (264-491) and the best block (596+) list none of them, and _AUTO_PROP_PREFIXES (190-258) has no hard_/would_/cs_ prefix, so _propagate_prefixed_metrics (251-258) drops them. Grep=0 in shadow_dispatcher.py confirmed.

But IMPACT is overstated/refuted on three counts: (1) hard_tier_bag_cap is NOT the R6 thermal 35/40 cap. common.py:1235 HARD_TIER_BAG_CAP={gold:6,std+:6,std:5,slow:4,new:4} with bag_after=len(bag)+1 is a per-tier bag-SIZE/order-count guard (B_load patology), unrelated to the R6 minute cap. The R6 tier-aware thermal observability the finding claims is lost is actually serialized: r6_max_bag_time_min (308/642), r6_per_order_violations (319/651), r6_picked_up_violations (320/652). So 'R6 cap is TIER-AWARE cannot be audited' is false. (2) Flag is LIVE: flags.json ENABLE_HARD_TIER_BAG_CAP=true, hot-loaded via C.load_flags() at feasibility_v2.py:464; when the cap fires it returns NO with reason 'hard_tier_bag_cap (tier bag_after>cap)' which IS serialized via the reason/feasibility_reason field, capturing tier+bag count+cap on the rejecting candidate. The pre-flip shadow-validation rationale is moot since the gate is already on. Real but minor metric-not-serialized hygiene gap; only the structured boolean for non-rejecting near-cap candidates is missing, and it is recoverable. P3, not P2.

title: post_shift_overrun_min/post_shift_overrun_penalty: computed always with explicit 'ZAWSZE widoczność w shadow' intent, leads best_effort selection when flipped, yet never serialized
evidence: dispatch_pipeline.py:5040-5044 comment: 'ZAWSZE (widoczność w shadow); wpływ na score/selekcję best_effort tylko gdy ENABLE_POST_SHIFT_OVERRUN_PENALTY'. Computed at 5052-5053, placed in enriched_metrics at 5430: "post_shift_overrun_min": post_shift_overrun_min. _post_shift_overrun_penalty_of (552-561) reads m.get("post_shift_overrun_penalty") and _best_effort_sort_key (591-592) returns (ps_pen, r6_pov, sla, bucket, -score, dur) — ps_pen is the LEADING tuple element. grep both keys in shadow_dispatcher.py = 0; 'post_shift_' is not a prefix in _AUTO_PROP_PREFIXES. Flag default OFF (common.py:1800 env '0').
impact: This is a 2026-06-24 feature in the live-decision flag registry (common.py:140). When ENABLE_POST_SHIFT_OVERRUN_PENALTY is flipped ON, the best_effort winner is chosen PRIMARILY by post_shift_overrun_penalty — but that value is absent from shadow_decisions, so you cannot reconstruct why a best_effort winner was chosen. While still OFF, the metric is computed expressly for shadow pre-flip validation, but that validation cannot consume shadow_decisions because the key is dropped. Direct contradiction between the code comment's stated intent and actual serialization.
protocol_note: Compute-always-for-shadow metric that defeats ETAP 5 replay-validation because it never lands in shadow_decisions. Before flipping ENABLE_POST_SHIFT_OVERRUN_PENALTY, the metric must first be serialized; otherwise the 'dowód pozytywnego wpływu' step has no data source.
recommendation: Confirm via dispatch_pipeline.py:5040-5053 and 588-592 that ps_pen leads the best_effort sort; add post_shift_overrun_min + post_shift_overrun_penalty to both serializer locations BEFORE any shadow-replay or flip of the penalty flag.

verification.reasoning: Re-verified all cited lines. post_shift_overrun_min/_penalty are computed unconditionally (dispatch_pipeline.py:5043-5053, comment 'Metryka liczona ZAWSZE (widocznosc w shadow)') and placed unconditionally in enriched_metrics (5429-5430). They are NOT serialized: shadow_dispatcher.py:190-258 _AUTO_PROP_PREFIXES has no 'post_shift_' prefix; _serialize_candidate (264+) is an explicit-whitelist + prefix-propagation serializer with no explicit post_shift field; grep post_shift_overrun shadow_dispatcher.py = 0. Empirical confirmation: grep -c post_shift_overrun logs/shadow_decisions.jsonl = 0 over 282 live records. _best_effort_sort_key (591-592) does put ps_pen as the LEADING tuple element via _post_shift_overrun_penalty_of (552-561, flag-gated, 0.0 when OFF), so when the flag flips ON the best_effort winner is led by a value that never reaches the log. Stronger than claimed: tools/post_shift_overrun_forward_replay.py is a dedicated consumer reading c.get('post_shift_overrun_penalty') from best/alternatives (lines 37-44,87); since the key is absent its any_pen_field guard is always False, every record buckets into no_pen and with_pen stays 0 - the GO/NO-GO flip verdict it exists to produce can never be computed. Flag ENABLE_POST_SHIFT_OVERRUN_PENALTY is OFF (common.py:1800 env '0', absent from flags.json), so no live mis-decision today, but this is exactly anti-pattern #4 (metric computed, not serialized) with a concrete silently-broken validation gate. P2 stands: observability/flip-validation impact, not a live HARD/SOFT correctness break.

title: R1/R5/R8 HARD-rule violation magnitudes (r1_violation_km, r5_violation_km, r8_violation_min) are placed in enriched_metrics but never serialized — siblings of the serialized spread/span metrics
evidence: feasibility_v2.py:505 metrics["r1_violation_km"] = round(spread_km - R1_MAX_DELIV_SPREAD_KM, 2); :574 metrics["r5_violation_km"] = round(pickup_spread_km - R5_MAX_MIXED_PICKUP_SPREAD_KM, 2); :642 metrics["r8_violation_min"] = round(span_min - hard_cap, 2). dispatch_pipeline.py re-asserts them into enriched_metrics: 5211 "r1_violation_km": metrics.get(...), 5212 "r5_violation_km", 5337 "r8_violation_min". impact: The exact amount by which a candidate breaches the R1 (deliv spread ≤8km), R5 (pickup spread) and R8 (pickup span) HARD limits is lost. verification.reasoning: Mechanical serialization-gap CONFIRMED, impact REFUTED. CONFIRMED: r1_violation_km/r5_violation_km/r8_violation_min sit in enriched_metrics (dispatch_pipeline.py:5125 builds enriched_metrics = {**metrics, ...}, plus redundant explicit re-asserts at 5211/5212/5337; computed at feasibility_v2.py:505/574/642). Neither shadow_dispatcher serializer LOCATION A (_serialize_candidate 264-489) nor LOCATION B (best block 496-864) emits them: both cherry-pick an explicit field list serializing the spread/span siblings (deliv_spread_km 304/638, pickup_spread_km 305/639, r8_pickup_span_min 371/706) but NOT the violation keys. _propagate_prefixed_metrics (254-261) auto-props only _AUTO_PROP_PREFIXES (190-199), which excludes r1_/r5_/r8_, and there is no wholesale **m / metrics:m dump (grep=0). So the gap is factually real. BUT impact ('exact breach amount is lost') is FALSE: (1) magnitudes are trivially recoverable as serialized_raw_measure minus a STATIC constant (R1_MAX_DELIV_SPREAD_KM=8.0 feasibility_v2:90; R5_MAX_MIXED_PICKUP_SPREAD_KM=2.5 :95; PICKUP_SPAN_HARD_BUNDLE2/3_MIN=15/30 common.py:729-730; bag_size also logged), and a real downstream tool already reconstructs exactly this (eod_drafts/2026-06-21/rule_weights_sensitivity.py:49 'Derive r8_violation_min from logged span + bag size'); (2) the violations are pure telemetry, NOT decision variables (feasibility_v2:495 'metryki do telemetrii ... reject NIE nastepuje'); (3) the decision-relevant SOFT penalties ARE serialized: bonus_r1_soft_pen/bonus_r5_soft_pen via 'bonus_' auto-prop prefix and bonus_r8_soft_pen explicitly (shadow_dispatcher 375/710). title: _KNOWN_XFAIL_SCRIPTS whole-subprocess xfail permanently masks NEW regressions in the proposal-selection golden and sticky-sequence read tests
